trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch with 1h budget and 30 iterations. Goal is ETTh1 720-step multivariate forecasting. Analysis: DLinear with RevIN is a strong, lightweight baseline that trains in seconds and is easy to implement correctly. Decision: Implement DLinear+RevIN, inspect the raw CSV format, train for 5 epochs, run local predict.py to verify the pipeline, and submit eval immediately to get baseline MSE and iteration time.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 1 failed with a fatal CUDA kernel error on a basic torch.mean operation, indicating the pre-installed PyTorch wheel is incompatible with the container GPU architecture. Analysis: We cannot reinstall PyTorch (no network), so we must fall back to CPU. DLinear has <100k parameters and the dataset is small; CPU training will be fast enough (seconds per epoch). Decision: Force CPU in train.py and predict.py, reduce DataLoader overhead (num_workers=0, pin_memory=False), suppress per-epoch stdout to save tokens, run a quick 5-epoch training, validate predict.py locally with dummy data, and immediately submit eval to get a baseline MSE and iteration time measurement.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 2 achieved MSE 0.4925 with DLinear (shared, no decomposition) + RevIN on CPU for 5 epochs. Training loss (~8.8) and validation loss (~12.5) were still decreasing, indicating underfitting and room for improvement. The 5-epoch run took ~9 min, suggesting per-batch CPU overhead is high with batch_size=32. Analysis: (1) Increasing batch size to 512 will cut DataLoader overhead and better utilize CPU BLAS. (2) Switching to individual linear layers lets each of the 7 features learn its own 96→720 mapping, which should help because features have very different scales (OT ~20-30 vs others ~1-5). (3) Training longer (25 epochs) with ReduceLROnPlateau and weight decay should push validation loss lower without manual tuning. (4) All of this stays well under the parameter budget. Decision: Update train.py with larger default batch size, weight decay, and LR scheduler; run 25 epochs with individual=True and bs=512; verify local predict.py; submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3 failed with a simple PyTorch API error: ReduceLROnPlateau does not accept 'verbose' in this version. Analysis: This is a trivial fix. The underlying training plan (individual linear layers, batch_size 512, 25 epochs, weight decay, LR scheduler) is sound and should improve upon the 0.4925 MSE baseline. Decision: Remove the verbose keyword from ReduceLROnPlateau, rerun training with --individual, verify predict.py locally, and submit eval.

trajectory.command:
cp checkpoints/best.pt checkpoints/best_baseline.pt && cp checkpoints/best.pt checkpoints/submit.pt && python train.py --individual --decomp --batch_size 2048 --epochs 25 && python predict.py --data_path dummy_test --checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt --output dummy_out.json && python -c "
import json, os
log_dir = os.path.join('logs', f'iter_{os.environ[\"RAB_ITERATION\"]}')
metrics = []
with open(os.path.join(log_dir, 'metrics.jsonl')) as f:
    for line in f:
        metrics.append(json.loads(line))
best_new = min(m['val_loss'] for m in metrics)
print(f'Best new val loss: {best_new:.6f}')
if best_new < 12.415627:
    os.system('cp checkpoints/best.pt checkpoints/submit.pt')
    print('Using new model')
else:
    os.system('cp checkpoints/best_baseline.pt checkpoints/submit.pt')
    print('Using baseline model')
" && echo "LOCAL_PREDICT_OK"

trajectory.description: Observation: The individual linear + RevIN model achieved MSE 0.484 on iteration 4. The training curve shows validation loss still slowly declining after 25 epochs, suggesting room for improvement, but the model is very simple and may be hitting a capacity plateau. Analysis: The original DLinear paper uses a moving-average decomposition into seasonal and trend components before applying linear projections. ETTh1 has strong daily seasonality (hourly data) that decomposition should exploit. Adding this while keeping the lightweight CPU-friendly design is the most promising next step. To stay within the tight ~30-minute budget, I will increase batch size to 2048 to cut per-epoch overhead dramatically (previous 512-bs run took ~40s/epoch; 2048 should take ~15s/epoch, so 25 epochs ≈ 6–8 min). I will also back up the current best checkpoint and automatically compare validation losses after training, submitting whichever checkpoint is stronger.

trajectory.error_summary: [TIMEOUT] Command exceeded 214s limit. Consider reducing epochs or using a smaller model.
trajectory.description: Observation: Best MSE is 0.484 with individual DLinear+RevIN. Decomposition did not help. Validation loss plateaued around 12.4. ~18 minutes remain. Analysis: The linear model is likely capacity-limited. Adding a lightweight cross-feature MLP (TSMixer-style) after the temporal projection introduces non-linearity and feature interaction while keeping parameters <500k. Training on combined train+val with a chronologically held-out validation split should improve generalization. To avoid regressing, we'll evaluate the old baseline on the same validation split and automatically submit whichever checkpoint performs better. Decision: Extend DLinear with optional residual MLP, update train.py for combined-data training and baseline comparison, update predict.py to handle the new flag, train 50 epochs, and submit eval.
